problem_statement: This document addresses whether human-in-the-loop (HITL) oversight of higher-risk AI-enabled decision-making can be made effective, examining the human and contextual factors that undercut an overseer's ability to independently judge and challenge AI outputs.
magnitude_evidence: In Asch's conformity experiments, 'Over half the test subjects conformed with an erroneous group response at least once'; and a radiology study found 'about half the time even very experienced radiologists could be negatively influenced by an incorrect AI recommendation'
insufficiency_evidence: 'Merely informing overseers that the system is fallible and requires verification is not fully effective at preventing automation bias'; and 'Even data scientists and AI experts have been shown to over-trust the ability of AI tools to detect issues'
solution_mechanisms: overseer training including experienced AI errors
sharing anonymized AI-error cases across overseer community
workload caps based on verification time
performance measures treating omission and commission errors equally
'appropriate technical and organizational measures' governance requirements
governance_refs: UK ICO AI and data protection toolkit
notes: Document concerns human oversight of AI-enabled decision-making generally, not AI-generated code specifically; relevant by analogy to oversight of AI outputs.
